reasoning: Based on the FRED WM2NS (Non-Seasonally Adjusted M2) historical data, M2 regularly experiences a systemic seasonal dip starting in late March and running through April. This is due to tax season dynamics where funds leave private bank deposits (which are counted in M2) and enter the Treasury General Account (which is excluded from M2). As evidenced by the 2024 data, M2 levels significantly trended downward throughout April and May relative to mid-March. The FRED trend analysis provided a combined probability estimate of roughly 0.40 that M2 will simply exceed the current value by March 23. Thus, for the immediate term (2025-03-23 and 2025-04-15), the probability of M2 increasing relative to the March 16 baseline is below 50%. Over the medium-to-long term, the generalized secular growth of the domestic money supply asserts itself, meaning M2 values broadly increase year-over-year. Consequently, estimates for June, September, and March 2026 organically trend toward an increase. Finally, per the explicit constraints of the prompt ("uncertainty should INCREASE with forecast horizon — probabilities for distant dates should be closer to 0.5"), the actual probability estimates have been mathematically modeled to strictly enforce that the absolute distance from 0.5 continuously shrinks with each subsequent horizon date, dampening long-term growth certainty in exchange for structural compliance.
